item.title: But you know who we need?
item.content:encoded:
i'll give you a hint: who goes to hell time and time again?
With guns? And kills everything? Again and again?
With more guns?

Jokes aside, your interpretation of what science should be (or, in your words, "actually" is) is quite conformist. Youre asserting that the scientific process of understanding mustnt let "be above all forms of knowledge", but in turn you also require that the scientific method complies with your wordview that the ancient "knew what they were doing" by, now explicating the parallel to the article, merely containing it but not understanding.
Balancing of the three pillars of "our beloved Foundation" is not simple, as it isnt easy doing science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best1 part about this is that not once in thousands of years did the "idiot ball" show up.

Everyone from generals to kings, popes to scientists; took one look at the very obvious portal to hell and said "I think we should let them keep praying."
A few of the very smart ones said "Send someone over to pray with them."

Best yet, the Foundation showed up, saw millennia of successful theological containment, and didn't try to replace it with unalloyed secular SCIENCE!
Instead they apply the actual scientific method and say "Does it work better if they pray like this? How about like this?"

I should keep this bookmarked as an example for new authors on how to characterize Foundation researchers well.

Footnotes
1. and sadly, refreshing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As much as I like pretty much everything by Spikebrennan, I think this article suffers from the fact, that a big part of it is basically just a long list of holly people and I don't think cutting it by half and saying that the list is abridged would hurt the article any, and it would probably improve it. That being said it's still incredibly satisfying and funny to me, especially the first half of the containment log.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The second image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), as it was created by micro-engraver Graham Short (as shown here), with no indication it was released under Creatives Commons. As such, that image has been removed. the other two images are cc-compliant, as the first one comes from NASA which is Public Domain (source here), and the second one comes from Wikimedia Commons and is licensed under CC BY-SA 2.0 (source here).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about having a scientific unit of piety just rubs me the wrong way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why you went with a leader of the RLDS church versus a leader of the less crazy, makes better sense LDS church like one of the Seventies or an Apostle. Still, neat article. Especially the historical entries.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: Approved. Nice job.

item.title: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

To this article we will be adding two footnotes containing crosslinks to relevant articles/task-forces. These articles are either A) directly related to the nature of the containment procedures or B) narratively build off this article, and thus crosslinks benefit the article proper.

Editing this:

The capsule’s ablative heat shield integrity is to be thoroughly inspected and repaired if necessary. The capsule is to be provisioned with food, drinking water, breathing air and requisite equipment for the conduct of the containment protocols by the relevant task force members.

and

1955 CE Containment breach leads to loss of capsule and its personnel. Secondary containment measure prevents several entities from escaping from SCP-1844's interior.

To this:

The capsule’s ablative heat shield integrity is to be thoroughly inspected and repaired if necessary. The capsule is to be provisioned with food, drinking water, breathing air and requisite equipment1 for the conduct of the containment protocols by the relevant task force members.

and

1955 CE Containment breach leads to loss of capsule and its personnel.2 Secondary containment measure prevents several entities from escaping from SCP-1844's interior.

Footnotes
1. Including a set of noise canceling headphones developed by Mobile Task Force Eta-11 and an audio amplification device engineered via study of SCP-2167.
2. Three members were eventually returned to the Foundation via SCP-2336. Treatment for extreme traumatic stress is still ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am sorry, but -1. This was too long and full of "Facts" and info for me to enjoy it. The SCP itself is not bad, but the story is not even a story. It is a list of things about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still one of my favorite SCPs. I would upvote this even if it was just a listing of Task Force Omega-144, because I love how you took the time and the effort to (very accurately) incorporate the beliefs and liturgies of so many different Abrahamic and Abrahamic-derived religious sects into this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Worth a necropost. Blame the new SCP-2336!

This is dazzlingly excellent. Ferocious atheist speaking!

How to make a good story with technological, religious, logical, funny, serious, food-for-thought aspects out of the (rather silly, if you ask me, considering what it actually is) moniker 'Door to Hell'.

What I particularly like about this SCP is the depiction of time passing. Most SCPs are a moment in time, whether safe or deadly. This containment has been going on forever, and getting better all the time. Or… getting too good, which is a nice final touch.

One of these skips which one wishes to upvote several times!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought they were 0 font size hidden messages and was disappointed, I have a weakness for those.
Also, I read everything you type in Peridot's voice. It's as adorable as it is annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no hard rule against it, although some authors have a preference for writing and separating sentences with double spaces. I myself am guilty of fixing this stylistic choice across the site, though I try not to do it once I've learned that it's a stylistic thing. In this author's case, it is. That being said, the triple spaces may be typos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgive my ignorance if this intentional, but Ctrl+F finds 40 cases of double spaces and 6 triple spaces. Does this need to be fixed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An Akiva is a somewhat obscure proposed measurement unit for degree of religious fervor/sincerity of belief.

All of the texts and prayers listed deal with the end of days, prayers for mercy, forms of exorcism (rites for rebuking malevolent entities and influences), and declarations of faith in the superiority of the divine over death and evil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was very hard to access the material in this without knowing much about religion. What are the common themes between the dozen or more religious texts you've mentioned? What's an akiva? (https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Akiva only has it as a name). No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good article (if confusing in spots), but that picture of the burning hole in the ground sucked me right out of it since I know where it's from, and I consider the real-life thing to be much more intriguing, somehow. So no-vote, but this is very well-done otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May i ask what the hell happened to the formatting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch. Fix'd. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure. The author is still active as far as I remember; how about sending them a PM asking about this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
JS124-143 — Rev. ██████ █████████
JS124-143 — Lady Mujtahideh ███████ █████

CV1666-621 — Archbishop ███████ ███████████
CV1666-621 — Mother Superior █████ █████████

Is it an error or is it intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a thing what makes monsters and you have to pray to keep it closed. For me, the engineering approach angle doesn't sufficiently interest me. The Foundation approaches everything as an engineering problem.

Also, the photo is of that literal perpetually burning hole in the ground (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Door_to_Hell) so that spoilt it a little for me too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. Super-religious SCPs seem like a relic, but the ultra-scientific language saves it. The tone is really nothing short of perfect. This is an excellent SCP, and I'm glad to see that concepts as familiar as "gateway to hell" are still fresh and interesting even as we're approaching 2000. Great work, and an enthusiastic upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally I don't go for the heavy religious SCPs, but this one is really well done. The logs and addendum at the end really solidify the idea of this phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is a lot of things I normally dislike - it's Abrahamic, the containment procedures are longer than I'd like, and a gate to hell was something that had me set to downvote it - but it's pulled off so well that I'm left conflicted. No vote for now, but I might come back and upvote later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love this. Portal to Hell done super well. (Rhyme time?) Have all the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised at you all. All this time, three pages of commentary, and no one has mentioned the great dilemma of that last collapsible - that unless they ease up on containment, normally anathema to the Foundation, all Hell's going to break loose…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm honestly surprised the author didn't write Horizon into this one in a joint effort with the Foundation. It seems right up their alley. +1 regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And 1844 is rated higher than [[SCP-616]]. I declare victory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: However, that one bugged me, in terms of semantics - if anything, War should've been released along with Conquest. But, bah, details, it was an entertaining read. The level of detail in containment measures is spectacular, and the clincher makes it even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my…I took one look at the 1939 entry…the thought that the Foundation may have unwittingly started WWII, AND released the First Horseman of the Apocalypse…Brilliant. Take my upvote, because now we know the Foundation doesn't even have to try to start Armegeddon, it just has to let it escape from this pit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that it's not classified as Keter is part of the whole point: the Foundation's official position is that they have successfully worked out a dependable way to keep this thing under control in perpetuity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it kills people and screws things over and breaches primary containment regularly, yeah, it should probably be Keter, but if they have it almost under control, Euclid is fair game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My takeaway: conceptual similarities to other SCPs don't matter much when the actual hook is different. When, in addition, the writing is this good, they're irrelevant. This has no business being anything but Keter, but otherwise I have no complaints. Nice hell hole you got here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very much upvoted!
One small remark, though: "Chabad" {חב"ד} are a khasidut, so they don't fall within the definition of "orthodox".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just did. Yeah, still doesn't move the needle for me.

Then again, I'm sort of reminded of seeing The Exorcist years ago when it was re-released. I said to the friend with whom I went to it that it didn't really impress me, and he pointed out that I wasn't raised Catholic (or in any religion).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't you mean 354? I remember 345 and its designated number better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was initially going to downvote this due to it being just some pit into hell that clergypeople have to keep contained. But the way you've explained this particular aspect got me to upvote. That's really clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did I say I love you already?
Now I kinda wish theological engineering was a thing, and that's me, an atheist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, true. This is of course because of the origin of the word. In Hebrew, שטן means an adversary or opponent, and in the Old Testament refers either to a number of human characters (without the definite article) or to a member of the divine counsel (in the definite article). Of course, like in other languages, the word usually refers to the arch-typical devil nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In case it isn't sufficiently obvious from the content and organization of the write-up, the fundamental idea of this piece is not that the containment subject itself is a unique new idea. The subject is very much what you think it is, and the religious aspects are intentionally presented to be taken at face value. The description is deliberately short, and I intended for the historical write-up to supply a sufficient sketch of the missing details. The concept may remind you of SCP-616, but this piece approaches that concept from the opposite direction. (In fact, one of my early drafts of this piece was intended as a re-write of 616).

The fundamental idea of this piece is how this particular threat is approached by the Foundation: engineers addressing an engineering problem. It's very much the conceptual brother of SCP-1512.

I can't tell you how much it annoys me when an SCP article contains religious references that were obviously drafted by someone who didn't bother to do the research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Centiakivas are very much still there.

For readers who may not have a background in theological engineering: the akiva (named after http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rabbi_Akiva ), is the ISU unit for fervor. To measure it, one places the subject three meters from a properly calibrated thaumaturgic meter (taking care to perform the measurement in an environment free from thaumaturgically reflective surfaces, such as silver or certain types of minerals such as marble, that may interfere with an accurate reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I intentionally put the mainstream religions on equal footing with the snake-handling preacher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If a single religion were able to contain it, they probably wouldn't shut up about it. But the fact that they all have to work together, and all have to visibly demonstrate that the others are just as true (for the purposes of containing this thing, at least) makes it more trouble than it's worth for there to be public knowledge. As Lumancer alludes to, showing that you can contain devils isn't worth it when you have to show the other guys can as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm delighted that the Abrahamic Super Friends have finally found a home. I'll agree with the sentiment that you've downplayed the object and its effects, but frankly that works in your favor here- I can't think of any way to increase detail without channeling either 354 or 616 to its detriment. Personally, I'm happy with it as it stands, and I'm definitely in the camp that liked the end notes.

Academically speaking, I'm once again intrigued by your choice of prayers for the Catholic members. The Nicene Creed is especially interesting since it's not a prayer, but a profession of faith- the religious equivalent of, say, reading the preamble of the American constitution aloud. I was also going to comment on the use of the exorcism rite (it's almost always used on people from what I remember), but I forgot just how little information the general public is given on the subject- this is the entire catechism entry on exorcism in the conventional sense, and the actual text in the Rituale Romanum is restricted to the clergy. What we do know is just vague enough to support how it's used here.

Overall, I like what you've done with it. Congratulations on finally getting this to gel- it's been a long time coming!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A more literal translation would be "one who is astray," or "rebel." Though it's generally understood to simply mean "devil."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Methinks the fact that multiple competing religions can all do the trick would shut them all up pretty fast. The potential popularization of religious relativism that would follow would be crippling for the smaller groups and foundation-shattering for the larger ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure how to feel about this. It's a lot of well-drafted, well-detailed work crafted around an ancient portal to hell.

It's sort of like like that episode of Iron Chef when the expert made four-star hot dogs with ketchup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good enough, but I actually really dislike the final collapsible; it is so very, very predictable that it basically ruined the article for me, and shifted an upvote into a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well-researched but reminds me too much of http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-616

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My main concern with this is how it remained concealed before the Foundation came around? Certainly the Catholic Church or any other religious group wouldn't keep this quiet: this thing being known would singlehandedly convince the entire world that god, specifically the Catholic punish-everyone-in-a-pit god, specifically the god that can only be stopped by devout Catholics (or Muslims) praying god, existed. That would be a religious killer app, if you know what I'm saying.

Also, how do they manage to get the jewish people to pray with conviction? This image of hell being a big angry fiery pit filled with demons isn't really supported by mainstream jewish theology, at least that's what I thought. I would find it hard to pray with conviction while literally in a hell I didn't believe in…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I miss centikavas, but still upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the task force from the original submarine tentacle, isn't it? In spirit, if nothing else.

I'm not sure how to feel about this. There's a lot wrong with it: requiring people to pray 'with conviction' to keep it contained (something that continuously sank the submarine tentacle, I should add), the lack of full description in the description, and a certain disconnect as far as the significance of the measurements. I mean, 9600 K is over 16,000 F, but I really don't know what that means, or how deep the thing is. (A quick Google search reveals that Jericho, the 'lowest town on earth', is 260 meters below sea level.)

That said, there's a lot to like. I tend to skip containment protocols anymore, but I read these and found them somewhat fascinating, despite the lingering dread of "oh, it's another thing that is contained by prayer". As long as this is, it's impeccably crafted. The history of containment is something I've never really seen before and I appreciate it.

Above all else, the final note in that last addendum is what got me to upvote, entirely due to the director's theory as to what's going on. That's actually pretty terrifying. I just wish, above all else, there was more to go on, and sooner, as to what this thing does when uncontained aside from "some old dead guys say it's filled with demons".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm getting the feeling that not everyone is opening the second collapsible at the bottom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well written, but also it's your standard pit to hell whence the bad things are held back with prayer.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes! The super god bothering task force is back! I missed them from the submarine infected with demonic tentacles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last recovered image of Agent ███████ during breach incident 22-██.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It kinda bugs me how the description doesn't actually describe what the SCP does. I know you pretty much tell what it does in the log, but it still feels strange. Besides that, for a straight forward Abrahamic SCP, it's…actually not bad. I think I like it enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it's certainly much improved from the original version, I'm still not entirely sure how I feel about this. It just seems too little attention is given to the pit's actual effects, in my opinion, and too much is given to the containment procedure.

As a side note, you have a slight translation error: "Whisperer of Evil" (شيطان)"- the word شيطان usually refers to the devil, and not the more general "evil" you described. I believe the word شر would be better suited for that. As I see it, you can either change the English translation or the Arabic word, either would work fine.
